methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $27M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing

private.name: planqc
private.rationale: Same sector (Quantum Computing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
QuantWare, a Series A quantum computing company founded by Matthijs Rijlaarsdam, has raised $27M to date. The quantum computing sector comprises 11 comparable companies (3 public, 8 private), including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion. These serve as the reference universe for valuation assessment.

Stage-progression methodology is applicable here. Using the $27M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to subsequent-stage advancement yields an implied valuation of $54M. The peer-median approach is not applicable: while 7 same-sector peers exist, insufficient datapoints met narrow filtration criteria (same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier alignment), making a comparable-company median unreliable. A broader sector median would introduce uncontrolled heterogeneity and was therefore excluded.

Triangulation across the single reliable method supports a valuation range of $46M (low), $54M (median), and $62M (high). The narrow methodological basis—relying on stage-progression alone—represents the primary uncertainty. Tighter valuation confidence would require either (1) additional private comps at QuantWare's stage with disclosed valuations, or (2) stronger market evidence that the 2.0x multiplier reflects current Series A pricing in quantum computing. Current risk factors include sector-level valuation volatility and the limited public comp liquidity.
